title: Ruptured Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
oneLiner: Sudden, catastrophic haemorrhage from a weakened aortic wall; almost exclusively in men >65 who smoke – minutes count, mortality >80% if missed.

sections.Ruptured Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (rAAA: **One-liner:** Sudden, catastrophic haemorrhage from a weakened aortic wall; almost exclusively in men >65 who smoke – minutes count, mortality >80% if missed.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute myocardial infarction (inferior/right ventricular MI)** – presents with epigastric pain, nausea, hypotension. **Single finding:** ECG showing ST elevation in II, III, aVF or new right bundle branch block (RBBB) with hypotension. **Don’t treat as “pancreatitis”** – get ECG first.
- **Acute aortic dissection (Type A)** – tearing chest/back pain, pulse deficit, aortic regurgitation. **Single finding:** Blood pressure difference >20 mmHg between arms or widened mediastinum on CXR. **If you hear an early diastolic murmur, call cardiothoracics immediately.**
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden, severe central abdominal or lower back pain (“ripping” or “tearing” quality) – often radiating to the flank or groin.
  - Syncope or near-syncope (early sign of haemorrhagic shock).
  - Abdominal distension + tenderness.
  - Pulsatile mass on palpation (if not obese; absent in 30–40% of ruptures).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg in late rupture) – but *contained rupture* may present with normal BP initially.
  - Cold, mottled extremities (shock).
  - Peritonism if intra-abdominal rupture (guarding, rigidity) – rare; retroperitoneal rupture is more common (dull, poorly localised pain, no peritonism).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Acute myocardial infarction** – ECG changes, raised troponin, no pulsatile mass.
  - **Acute pancreatitis** – epigastric pain + vomiting + raised lipase (>3x ULN); no hypotension until later.
  - **Perforated peptic ulcer** – board-like rigidity, free gas under diaphragm on erect CXR.
  - **Renal colic** – flank pain radiating to groin, haematuria, no shock unless infected.
  - **Mesenteric ischaemia** – pain out of proportion to exam, atrial fibrillation, lactate high.
- **Splitter:** Pulsatile abdominal mass (≥5 cm) + hypotension = rAAA until proven otherwise. Absence of mass does not rule it out – proceed to CT if high suspicion.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour in ED):**
  - Focused abdominal ultrasound by emergency physician (FAST) – look for retroperitoneal haematoma or aortic aneurysm. **Do not rely on negative scan** – if body habitus poor or operator inexperienced, go straight to CT.
  - 12-lead ECG – exclude MI.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, clotting screen, lactate, cross-match (request 6 units group-specific blood immediately).
  - Point-of-care lactate – >4 mmol/L indicates severe hypoperfusion.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT angiography (aorta) – gold standard. If haemodynamically unstable, perform in CT only if surgical team ready and patient stable enough to transfer; otherwise go straight to theatre.
  - **Second-line:** Bedside ultrasound (if CT unavailable) – identifies aneurysm >5 cm, but cannot fully assess rupture.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Clinical triad: sudden abdominal/back pain + pulsatile mass + hypotension (present in <50%).
  - CT: aortic diameter >5.5 cm (men) or >5 cm (women) with extravasation of contrast.
- **Never-miss test:** **CT angiography of the aorta** – omission is fatal. If unstable, call vascular surgeon and move to theatre; do not waste time on other imaging.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Call vascular surgeon NOW** – do not wait for imaging.
  - **IV access** – two large-bore cannulae (14G or 16G) in antecubital fossae.
  - **Resuscitation:** **Permissive hypotension** – target systolic 70–90 mmHg (until cross-clamp). **Do not give excessive crystalloid** – use blood components early. Give O-negative blood if cross-match not ready.
  - **Analgesia:** Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV titrated to pain (beware hypotension – give small boluses). [Check local policy for exact dose if needed.]
  - **NBM** – immediate.
  - **Transfer to theatre** (open repair or endovascular – EVAR) as soon as possible.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Post-operative ICU/HDU care – monitor for bleeding, graft infection, compartment syndrome.
  - Maintain MAP >65 mmHg with fluids and vasopressors (noradrenaline if needed).
  - Correct coagulopathy (FFP, platelets as per local massive transfusion protocol).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If survival: CT surveillance of graft at 1 month, then annually.
  - Cardiovascular risk reduction – statin, antiplatelet (aspirin 75 mg daily), BP control, smoking cessation.
  - Consider screening for first-degree relatives (abdominal ultrasound).
- **Default Orders (if admitted to ward post-repair):**
  - **Fluids:** IV crystalloid (Hartmann’s) at maintenance rate; blood products as per haemoglobin and clotting.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily) – start when no active bleeding and renal function adequate.
  - **Diet:** NBM until bowel sounds return + no abdominal distension (usually 24–48h).
  - **Observations:** Hourly for first 6h post-op (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, urine output), then 4-hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Analgesia:** Morphine IV 2.5–5 mg every 5–10 min as needed (titrate to pain; avoid hypotension). [Check local policy.]
  - **Antibiotics (peri-operative):** Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV at induction (if aortic graft planned). Alternative: cefuroxime 1.5 g IV + metronidazole 500 mg IV. Follow local microbiology guidelines.
  - **Vasopressor (if hypotensive despite fluids):** Noradrenaline (norepinephrine) IV infusion – start at 0.05 mcg/kg/min, titrate to MAP >65. [Check local policy for preparation.]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Analgesia:** Fentanyl 25–50 mcg IV (shorter duration, less hypotension).
  - **Vasopressor:** Vasopressin 0.03–0.04 units/min (may be used in massive haemorrhage).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Morphine: avoid in severe respiratory depression (but rare in this setting); naloxone reversal available.
  - Noradrenaline: extravasation causes tissue necrosis – ensure central line if prolonged use.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min (e.g., 20 mg SC once daily) – consult pharmacist.
  - Morphine: caution in renal failure (active metabolite accumulates) – reduce dose or consider fentanyl.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving excessive crystalloid (e.g., 2L normal saline bolus) before surgical control** – this increases BP, dislodges clot, and accelerates haemorrhage. **Target permissive hypotension, not normotension.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Cardiac arrest** – from exsanguination.
  - **Acute kidney injury** – from hypovolaemia + cross-clamping.
  - **Compartment syndrome** (abdominal or lower limb) – consider if tense abdomen, raised bladder pressure, or limb pallor/pain after repair.
  - **Graft infection** – presents with fever, leucocytosis, pain – requires long-term antibiotics ± graft removal.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Graft occlusion** – limb ischaemia; needs duplex and potential thrombectomy.
  - **Aorto-enteric fistula** (rare) – haematemesis or melaena months–years post-repair – CT evidence of air around graft.
sections.Prognosis: - Overall mortality for rAAA: **80–90%** (pre-hospital + in-hospital). For those reaching theatre: 30–50% in-hospital mortality. If shock at presentation (systolic <90 mmHg), mortality >70%. Long-term survival for discharged patients is similar to matched controls if risk factors managed.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Know your local massive transfusion protocol** – activate it early (ratio 1:1:1 RBC:FFP:platelets).
- **If ultrasound shows AAA but patient is hypotensive, do not delay for CT** – transfer to theatre now.
- **Check NEWS2** – a single score of 7 (HR >130, BP <90, RR >30) should trigger immediate escalation to consultant and theatre.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give a short-acting opioid and send for CT** – patient may become hypotensive in scanner. Escort with anaesthetist if unstable.
- **Do not waste time on “routine” blood cultures or urine dipstick** – these will not change management in the first hour.
- **Do not assume absence of pulsatile mass excludes rAAA** – especially in obese or contained ruptures. If clinical suspicion high, proceed to imaging.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- *Rarely occurs – most patients are admitted. If patient declines surgery or has contained rupture and is haemodynamically stable:*
- **Return if:** New severe abdominal/back pain, syncope, or limb weakness/numbness.
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient CT angiogram within 1 week (if not already done). Refer to vascular surgeon for elective repair if AAA >5.5 cm. Arrange smoking cessation.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) immediately if:**
  - Suspected rAAA – even if stable on arrival.
  - NEWS2 ≥7 (any single parameter: HR >130, BP <90, RR >25, SpO2 <91% on air).
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L.
  - GCS <13 (unless sedated).
  - Systolic BP <90 mmHg despite 1 unit of blood.
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - Cardiac arrest (peri-arrest) – always consider reversible causes (hypovolaemia, tension pneumothorax from CPR?).
  - Ongoing haemorrhage with no response to initial resuscitation – need massive transfusion protocol and theatre.

rawMarkdown:
## Ruptured Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (rAAA)
**One-liner:** Sudden, catastrophic haemorrhage from a weakened aortic wall; almost exclusively in men >65 who smoke – minutes count, mortality >80% if missed.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute myocardial infarction (inferior/right ventricular MI)** – presents with epigastric pain, nausea, hypotension. **Single finding:** ECG showing ST elevation in II, III, aVF or new right bundle branch block (RBBB) with hypotension. **Don’t treat as “pancreatitis”** – get ECG first.
- **Acute aortic dissection (Type A)** – tearing chest/back pain, pulse deficit, aortic regurgitation. **Single finding:** Blood pressure difference >20 mmHg between arms or widened mediastinum on CXR. **If you hear an early diastolic murmur, call cardiothoracics immediately.**

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden, severe central abdominal or lower back pain (“ripping” or “tearing” quality) – often radiating to the flank or groin.
  - Syncope or near-syncope (early sign of haemorrhagic shock).
  - Abdominal distension + tenderness.
  - Pulsatile mass on palpation (if not obese; absent in 30–40% of ruptures).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg in late rupture) – but *contained rupture* may present with normal BP initially.
  - Cold, mottled extremities (shock).
  - Peritonism if intra-abdominal rupture (guarding, rigidity) – rare; retroperitoneal rupture is more common (dull, poorly localised pain, no peritonism).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Acute myocardial infarction** – ECG changes, raised troponin, no pulsatile mass.
  - **Acute pancreatitis** – epigastric pain + vomiting + raised lipase (>3x ULN); no hypotension until later.
  - **Perforated peptic ulcer** – board-like rigidity, free gas under diaphragm on erect CXR.
  - **Renal colic** – flank pain radiating to groin, haematuria, no shock unless infected.
  - **Mesenteric ischaemia** – pain out of proportion to exam, atrial fibrillation, lactate high.
- **Splitter:** Pulsatile abdominal mass (≥5 cm) + hypotension = rAAA until proven otherwise. Absence of mass does not rule it out – proceed to CT if high suspicion.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour in ED):**
  - Focused abdominal ultrasound by emergency physician (FAST) – look for retroperitoneal haematoma or aortic aneurysm. **Do not rely on negative scan** – if body habitus poor or operator inexperienced, go straight to CT.
  - 12-lead ECG – exclude MI.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, clotting screen, lactate, cross-match (request 6 units group-specific blood immediately).
  - Point-of-care lactate – >4 mmol/L indicates severe hypoperfusion.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CT angiography (aorta) – gold standard. If haemodynamically unstable, perform in CT only if surgical team ready and patient stable enough to transfer; otherwise go straight to theatre.
  - **Second-line:** Bedside ultrasound (if CT unavailable) – identifies aneurysm >5 cm, but cannot fully assess rupture.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Clinical triad: sudden abdominal/back pain + pulsatile mass + hypotension (present in <50%).
  - CT: aortic diameter >5.5 cm (men) or >5 cm (women) with extravasation of contrast.
- **Never-miss test:** **CT angiography of the aorta** – omission is fatal. If unstable, call vascular surgeon and move to theatre; do not waste time on other imaging.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Call vascular surgeon NOW** – do not wait for imaging.
  - **IV access** – two large-bore cannulae (14G or 16G) in antecubital fossae.
  - **Resuscitation:** **Permissive hypotension** – target systolic 70–90 mmHg (until cross-clamp). **Do not give excessive crystalloid** – use blood components early. Give O-negative blood if cross-match not ready.
  - **Analgesia:** Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV titrated to pain (beware hypotension – give small boluses). [Check local policy for exact dose if needed.]
  - **NBM** – immediate.
  - **Transfer to theatre** (open repair or endovascular – EVAR) as soon as possible.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Post-operative ICU/HDU care – monitor for bleeding, graft infection, compartment syndrome.
  - Maintain MAP >65 mmHg with fluids and vasopressors (noradrenaline if needed).
  - Correct coagulopathy (FFP, platelets as per local massive transfusion protocol).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If survival: CT surveillance of graft at 1 month, then annually.
  - Cardiovascular risk reduction – statin, antiplatelet (aspirin 75 mg daily), BP control, smoking cessation.
  - Consider screening for first-degree relatives (abdominal ultrasound).
- **Default Orders (if admitted to ward post-repair):**
  - **Fluids:** IV crystalloid (Hartmann’s) at maintenance rate; blood products as per haemoglobin and clotting.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily) – start when no active bleeding and renal function adequate.
  - **Diet:** NBM until bowel sounds return + no abdominal distension (usually 24–48h).
  - **Observations:** Hourly for first 6h post-op (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, urine output), then 4-hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Analgesia:** Morphine IV 2.5–5 mg every 5–10 min as needed (titrate to pain; avoid hypotension). [Check local policy.]
  - **Antibiotics (peri-operative):** Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV at induction (if aortic graft planned). Alternative: cefuroxime 1.5 g IV + metronidazole 500 mg IV. Follow local microbiology guidelines.
  - **Vasopressor (if hypotensive despite fluids):** Noradrenaline (norepinephrine) IV infusion – start at 0.05 mcg/kg/min, titrate to MAP >65. [Check local policy for preparation.]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Analgesia:** Fentanyl 25–50 mcg IV (shorter duration, less hypotension).
  - **Vasopressor:** Vasopressin 0.03–0.04 units/min (may be used in massive haemorrhage).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Morphine: avoid in severe respiratory depression (but rare in this setting); naloxone reversal available.
  - Noradrenaline: extravasation causes tissue necrosis – ensure central line if prolonged use.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min (e.g., 20 mg SC once daily) – consult pharmacist.
  - Morphine: caution in renal failure (active metabolite accumulates) – reduce dose or consider fentanyl.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving excessive crystalloid (e.g., 2L normal saline bolus) before surgical control** – this increases BP, dislodges clot, and accelerates haemorrhage. **Target permissive hypotension, not normotension.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Cardiac arrest** – from exsanguination.
  - **Acute kidney injury** – from hypovolaemia + cross-clamping.
  - **Compartment syndrome** (abdominal or lower limb) – consider if tense abdomen, raised bladder pressure, or limb pallor/pain after repair.
  - **Graft infection** – presents with fever, leucocytosis, pain – requires long-term antibiotics ± graft removal.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Graft occlusion** – limb ischaemia; needs duplex and potential thrombectomy.
  - **Aorto-enteric fistula** (rare) – haematemesis or melaena months–years post-repair – CT evidence of air around graft.

### Prognosis
- Overall mortality for rAAA: **80–90%** (pre-hospital + in-hospital). For those reaching theatre: 30–50% in-hospital mortality. If shock at presentation (systolic <90 mmHg), mortality >70%. Long-term survival for discharged patients is similar to matched controls if risk factors managed.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Know your local massive transfusion protocol** – activate it early (ratio 1:1:1 RBC:FFP:platelets).
- **If ultrasound shows AAA but patient is hypotensive, do not delay for CT** – transfer to theatre now.
- **Check NEWS2** – a single score of 7 (HR >130, BP <90, RR >30) should trigger immediate escalation to consultant and theatre.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give a short-acting opioid and send for CT** – patient may become hypotensive in scanner. Escort with anaesthetist if unstable.
- **Do not waste time on “routine” blood cultures or urine dipstick** – these will not change management in the first hour.
- **Do not assume absence of pulsatile mass excludes rAAA** – especially in obese or contained ruptures. If clinical suspicion high, proceed to imaging.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- *Rarely occurs – most patients are admitted. If patient declines surgery or has contained rupture and is haemodynamically stable:*
- **Return if:** New severe abdominal/back pain, syncope, or limb weakness/numbness.
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient CT angiogram within 1 week (if not already done). Refer to vascular surgeon for elective repair if AAA >5.5 cm. Arrange smoking cessation.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) immediately if:**
  - Suspected rAAA – even if stable on arrival.
  - NEWS2 ≥7 (any single parameter: HR >130, BP <90, RR >25, SpO2 <91% on air).
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L.
  - GCS <13 (unless sedated).
  - Systolic BP <90 mmHg despite 1 unit of blood.
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - Cardiac arrest (peri-arrest) – always consider reversible causes (hypovolaemia, tension pneumothorax from CPR?).
  - Ongoing haemorrhage with no response to initial resuscitation – need massive transfusion protocol and theatre.
